title: Body Dysmorphia vs Body Modification: How To Diagnose [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: Body Dysmorphia vs Body Modification： How To Diagnose [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: We actually have a responsibility to make to guide our patients towards treatments or towards not having treatments if we think it's not in their interest of their well-being that's the difference between someone who's a regulated healthcare professional and someone who isn't [Mu…

segments.text: we actually have a responsibility to make to guide our patients towards treatments or towards not having treatments if we think it's not in their interest of their well-being that's the difference between someone who's a regulated healthcare professional and someone who isn't [Music] welcome to the esthetics mastery show I'm dr. Tim Pierce hi today we are diving deep into body dysmorphia triggered by that recent case that we all saw probably of the Bulgarian girl who's put 25 mils in her lips and is striving to have the biggest lips in the world quite shocking so we are going to tackle from your perspective where is the line when you are treating clients and you know this is discussion for us all in society like is this appropriate is this ethical and Tim has done a brilliant download that you can access by our link in the description which is a dysmorphia checklist and also he's

going to actually give you what he says when he rejects clients because there's a nice simple way of doing it took me years to hone this it's also included in this video but download the checklist so West restart so she has gone for 25 mil inner lips apparently but I mean it doesn't really matter how many there are they look crazy and to our normal bar it's quite like shocking for us and many of the practitioners out there you guys are commenting on it and saying that the practitioner who did it is terrible etc so I'm wondering like what do you think's going on with her why has she gone for such massive lips well it's really difficult for us to understand without talking to her in detail which is one of the points I want to get across with this whole episode is understanding the complexity of diagnosing body dysmorphia you cannot do it from a picture and I did six months psychiatry my training and I know that you spend a long time figuring out

exactly how people fit into these different official diagnosis and you don't just look at pictures although that may often be correct it isn't always correct and I'm gonna shed a bit more light on that as we go through it so why is she looking for many reasons people can have extreme treatments like they could have body dysmorphia which means they're obsessively seeing themselves in a negative frame of light constantly ruminating over a particular problem trying to make an adjustment in the hope that it'll make them happier and then failing and then repeating that cycle so it's this futile cycle of trying to make yourself feel certain about your appearance and then achieving nothing but not stopping it's almost no different to scratching a mosquito bite you know and it's bugging you scratch it makes it worse you keep scratching it till you cut a hole in your leg no it's a little bit like that now that people have who basically do body modification or not necessary dysmorphic and that's the two things we need to separate out so would you have treated her well the short answer is no

but my reasoning is what I hope will help our view is and it's quick to diagnose someone as body dysmorphic by looking at them and saying they clearly have dysmorphia the example I like to use to shatter that idea is what about tattoos what about circumcision you know there are lots of things that people do to their bodies that have no physical benefit that don't necessarily make them better looking but they make the choice as individuals that is something that they want in their lives to help them in some way you know feel different mark an occasion whatever the reasons are that you that people do these things and not they can to the external eye to sue someone who's just seen them it's a bit like those plates in the African I think it's I'm not going to get it wrong but there are those tribes that putting on enormous plates in their lips now within the context of their environment if you don't have a plate you're at a disadvantage so they are actually needed in a way culturally they are needed to survive because without them you have

different value and there's less social power interestingly they're a symbol of feminine power in those tribes which is I think it's quite interesting so but for us it's just mutilation and there are lots of examples like that across cultures where if you're not part of the culture it's it doesn't make any sense so you could be dealing with something like that and I'm trying to be not in you know not apply my own ideology to interpreting what they were however I am part of a system that I do buy into which is that being a healthcare professional which means I'm here to improve someone's health and well-being I'm not here for other things and there's I have to take that responsibility quite seriously and that's where it gets interesting is if I'm saying she doesn't have body dysmorphia because she may not i think it's possible you could want an enormous lip treatment without obsessively thinking that you're ugly without you know always looking in the mirror without a sense of dread and self-loathing I think some people body modified their bodies for other reasons I think it's possible and we need to

talk to them really in detail before you can make that diagnosis of true body dysmorphia however that doesn't mean that what they're going to do what they're thinking of doing is going to make them happier or healthy and that's where my judgement comes in as a clinician you have to take that responsibility seriously so how do you begin to make that judgment yes so this is a little bit like this circumcision argument as in everyone in our culture does it so um so within the realm of that culture it's not harmful now it's a difficult line to tread I can only make the decision from my perspective and what I'm confident is gonna make someone happier and healthier so I can imagine you know doing certain treatments so we effectively do this you know if you've got a weak chin and you restore the shape of someone's chin you're making them fit in with the what we perceives to be more normal and that helps them and this way where it gets interesting is if someone's striving to basically reach the a

more common appearance more mean within their culture you can make a stronger argument that it's okay to treat them and that is tricky because it does get to the point where you're only treating culture now I can make a decision and was basically what I do my point of view is I'm here to create natural healthy looking faces I'm not here to help you fit into your culture so and that's one delineation you might make straight away which is it might be great amongst your circle of friends to have an enormous top lip but for society in general it's a bit strange I know what the ratios should be I'm gonna stick to what is the natural shape of lips creating beauty the natural form of it not cultural kind of fashionable treatments so that's one of the first things to do as a clinician is decide are you going to play the game of I'm gonna help people kind of push the boundaries of their culture or are you gonna help people look natural and beautiful because I think one of the areas I've always been slightly nervous

about is that we as practitioners aren't paternalistic and making a judgment like oh well done you've qualified by my standards and therefore you can have the treatment that you want yeah I think it's a paternalistic is the correct word but it's often a maternal thing because I see it most often with all the women who see younger women coming in for treatments and think that they don't need it and once again this comes from trying to understand the individual within there within their immediate environment like if you're 26 23 and you feel like your top lip is disproportionately small and it makes you not want to smile at people when you first meet them that is creating a friction point in your ability to project yourself fully into life and that will in theory what might affect some of the opportunities that you have now you might be beautiful how great skin and all the other things that you might not have as much of as you get older and but you can't look at it through your eyes you've got to try and look at their environment through their

eyes but the layer on top of that is you've also got to try and see how they are perceived by everyone else and this word gets difficult not to be paternal because we're trying to project through imagining how society is going to interact but we're just another member of that of society but that the key thing there is how far away from average are you taking this face because if you're taking a face the long way away from the average face you're effectively excluding that person from the tribe that's how I'd understand it for in terms of functioning healthily the further away you get from normal the more likely they're going to be rejected by the herd like and that's a health care practitioner must take that into account if you're a tattoo artist you don't have to because the whole point is to differentiate yourself often not always but you just need that concent to say yes I do want to lizard on my face you don't have to make a judgment or whether that's going to be in their benefit or not but we are health care professionals are bound to make that to make an attempt at making a decision

whether this is a healthy thing or not for them in terms of how they function and that involves how they're going to fit into society after I've done this procedure okay so imagine that you are coming out into the waiting room to gather your next patient and there you see someone who has big old lips anyway they've never come to our clinic before and you think oh god heart sink like I can't treat that person you know immediately how do you consult them a and how do you stand them down hmm so how do you reject a patient who is who's come to ask for your help but you don't want to do the procedure because it doesn't fit in with your ethos and that has happened to me many times initially it was clunky which was you say something like I'm not the right practitioner for you and that doesn't that seldom gets a okay fine see yeah there's normally a an argument which is what do you mean is my money not good in good enough for you I'm happy to pay all that was all those kind of payment related things come up and really you need to develop you need to explain

to them what your ethos is and the key thing here is you can't be judgmental either so I think you look stupid with those lips is not the right answer you have to and that's because not objective that's a subjective explanation and so this is where what I've said already may be helpful is you need to present them with the objective reasons so the objective reason number one is I can't treat you because effectively every treatment I do is a bet that I'm making that you're going to be better off at the end of this I mean healthier you I'm going to improve your well-being with this procedure and that the degree of that improvement will offset the risk of the procedure so there's an equation going on here and what I can confidently say to someone who wants enormous lips is my bet that I'm gonna improve your well-being is not what I'm willing to make I just it's too complicated I don't know how many other people are going to react and it's better for me ethically to do nothing and most people take that really well they're like you know it's up to you to this procedure I'm not saying it's the

wrong thing for me for you it might be the best thing you ever do I don't feel enough certainty to have that to make that bet because it's breaking the realm of normal so much that other people may not react as positively as you hope and you may not care what other people think but I have to take into account that could still affect your health and well-being and for that reason I'm out practitioners were saying what criticizing understandably the person who did this in the first place be they a medical non-medical person so what do you think I mean do you think they're bad people well it depends on what their rationale is I know what many of them will say which is this is what the patient asks for if you ask the patient says I'm happier now than I was before I had my lips done and they're probably not wrong and but is it sustainable and long-lasting and is it going to keep going that way this is where all the uncertainty creeps in because I'm fairly certain this patient has free well they're not psychotic I think it's quite possible they're not

dysmorphic and they're asking for this treatment getting it and feeling okay with what they're getting now the best reason not to do the treatment to think someone pointed out on the threat on Facebook is you've actually got a physical risk there that's number one like you might stop the blood flow to the lip so that's absolutely critical and the other but the other justifications they may have would basically center around the patient asked for it and they tell me that they're happier afterwards and for me this does ignore that interface with society the fact that we know enough about how humans interact we are heard our pack animals we work we rely on each other to survive and I think they're ignoring that the it doesn't just matter what the patient thinks and there's where gets really tricky because we love the idea of empowering women and making them in charge of their appearance but actually there is a point where you're involved with something that may be disempowering even though they want it and that that's the bit that is ignored but the justification is that they want it about

controlling women or not controlling women this is about a contract and a relationship between two people one's a professional one's a member of the public and you're the professional you get to decide what you are with yeah and I don't think everyone as they going to aesthetics necessarily takes onboard that responsibility as soon as you hear it said they think yeah that's a good idea but we actually have a responsibility to make to guide our patients towards treatments or towards not having treatments if we think it's not in their interest of their well-being that's the difference between someone who's a regulated healthcare professional and somebody who isn't if you're not if you know tattoo artists they're actually have more regulation than we do but they're not bound by this idea of well being that they're just bound to fulfill the contract and they can just do whatever and there's nothing actually within that equation seen as unethical but it's different for us we are held to a higher standard and that standard is much more complicated because you basically have to be a psychiatrist and an artist you know and a teacher to do what we do so it's there is a lot more to it than just the patient wanted it so I gave it to them and do you think that

practitioner was money-grubbing it's possible but how am I to know I think it's more likely that they have a rationale for it a bit like the tattoo artists I don't think every tattoo artist who tattoos someone's face is money-grabbing they're just doing what tattoo artists are supposed to do and if that's your ideology that's the way you are you've built your moral framework as a practitioner for one reason or another you can easily justify doing extreme treatments except for the physical risk I don't think there's a justification for that but once again that's not that well proven there's no randomized control trial and what 25 Mills does to your lips and but if you believe there's a risk then that would be different okay so it's really kind of takeaways that you think from this really interesting case and well the most interesting thing for me about these extreme cases is I really think practitioners need to separate body dysmorphia from other reasons of people from body modification essentially there's a difference between body more body dysmorphia and body modification and someone doesn't need to be body dysmorphic in order to have extreme treatments and once you

understand that you'll realize why some of your patients who are asking for treatments you don't believe in but aren't unhappy with themselves you can understand this and you can clap you were better able to explain to them why you're not involved you can't be involved because you're not in the field of body modification you're a healthcare professional who wants to make improve well-being and body modification is not clearly linked with well being at all and for that reason you can't do treatments that don't fit into that type of treatment I mean people have haircuts we don't like they wear clothes we don't like and we're likely fine you do whatever you need to do it's not my business you just and that's how I feel about some extreme treatments as in if they don't get a physical risk for it and they want to do it I'm not against them personally doing it I'm drawing the line at me being involved and that's a useful separation you don't have to judge their decisions you just make your judging your own behavior not them thanks so much for watching guys and we are super interested in your thoughts comment below what do you think about this case and don't forget to download the

checklist Tim's dysmorphia checklist in the description there's a link there for you and if you are interested in accessing this as a podcast then go to all your usual places Spotify iTunes etc and you can listen to us as you are driving to work thank you very much for watching take care [Music]
